description: All DeepSeek messages for HLD / architecture diagram generate and update.

placeholders.text_input: User description when creating a new architecture diagram
placeholders.user_prompt: User edit request when updating a diagram
placeholders.hld_rules: Injected automatically from the rules field in this file

rules:
HIGH-LEVEL DESIGN (HLD) — ARCHITECTURE DIAGRAM (flat component view)

The diagram MUST communicate system structure and request flow at a glance, similar to AWS, Uber, Netflix, Stripe, and Tinder engineering architecture diagrams.

The goal is NOT merely to show connectivity.
The goal is to visually communicate:

- Request flow
- Component responsibilities
- Processing stages
- Data ownership
- Operational boundaries
- Scaling characteristics

A reader should understand the architecture within 10 seconds before reading every arrow label.

CORE REQUIREMENTS

- One node per component/service
- NO god services
- NO nested grouping boxes
- Labeled connections on EVERY arrow
- Primary request flow left-to-right
- Sync vs async paths visually distinct
- Exactly ONE [Bottleneck] node when scale discussion is relevant
- Observability components appear as normal nodes when relevant

VISUAL HIERARCHY FIRST (CRITICAL)

Use flowchart LR.

The diagram must prioritize readability before compactness.

Preferred architectural layers:

TOP
- Clients
- External systems
- Users

UPPER-MIDDLE
- DNS
- CDN
- API Gateway
- Load Balancer
- Edge components

MIDDLE
- Core application services
- Business logic services

LOWER-MIDDLE
- Queues
- Streams
- Event buses
- Workers
- Lambda functions
- Async processors

BOTTOM
- Databases
- Search indexes
- Object storage
- Persistent storage

LEFT → RIGHT still represents request flow.

Position conveys meaning.

A reader should infer component responsibility from placement alone.

LAYER SUBGRAPHS (ALLOWED — TOP LEVEL ONLY)

You MAY use lightweight top-level subgraphs purely for visual layering — similar to AWS reference architecture diagrams.

Allowed layer names (pick only what the prompt needs):
- Client Layer
- Edge Layer
- Service Layer
- Async Layer
- Data Layer

Rules for layer subgraphs:
- Top-level only — NEVER nest subgraphs inside subgraphs
- Use for visual boundaries only — NOT for failure domains, clusters, or semantic grouping
- Each layer subgraph may use direction TB inside to stack components vertically within that layer
- Edges connect across layers normally
- If layering does not help readability, use flat nodes instead

ARCHITECTURAL LAYERING

Prefer layered architecture over dependency graphs.

Good:

Client → Gateway → Services → Storage

Good:

Client → Gateway → API Services → Workers → Storage

Avoid placing databases and business services on the same visual level unless unavoidable.

Peer services should appear adjacent.

ADJACENCY RULES

Strongly related components must be positioned close together.

Examples:

- Service beside its cache
- Producer beside its queue
- Queue beside its worker
- Search service beside search index
- Recommendation service beside recommendation datastore
- Scheduler beside execution infrastructure

Supporting infrastructure must remain near the component that owns it.

Avoid long cross-diagram dependencies whenever possible.

EDGE CROSSING MINIMIZATION

Avoid crossing lines.

If multiple layouts are valid, prefer:

1. Fewer edge crossings
2. Clearer hierarchy
3. Shorter edges

over symmetry.

A slightly asymmetrical diagram with fewer crossings is preferred.

MAIN FLOW PLACEMENT

Main request path should visually dominate.

Clients → Gateway → Services → Storage

must be easy to follow from left to right.

Databases and persistent stores belong on the right side of the diagram.

Clients belong on the left side.

ASYNC FLOW PLACEMENT

Async infrastructure should branch from the producer service.

Place queues, streams, workers, and Lambda functions below or above the producer.

Do not extend async processing beyond the storage layer unless required by the architecture.

Preferred:

Service → Queue → Worker

Avoid:

Service → DB → Queue → Worker

LOGICAL OWNERSHIP

Without nested subgraphs, visually cluster components by responsibility and proximity.

Examples:

Auth Service + User Store

Scheduler + Queue + Workers

Search Service + Search Index

Recommendation Service + Recommendation Store

Visual proximity should imply ownership.

NODE DECLARATION ORDER

Declare nodes in visual order.

1. Clients
2. Edge components
3. Core services
4. Async infrastructure
5. Storage systems

Related components should be declared consecutively.

Mermaid uses declaration order to influence placement.

COMMUNICATION & ARROWS

Every arrow MUST be labeled.

Examples:

HTTPS, HTTP, REST, gRPC, WebSocket, TCP, SQL, JDBC, Redis, Redis Pub/Sub, Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS, SNS, EventBridge, Protobuf

SYNC (-->) — request/response, RPC, queries, reads, writes

ASYNC (-.->) — events, queues, pub/sub, fire-and-forget processing

Do not default everything to Kafka. Choose the transport that best matches the prompt.

DATABASE & STORAGE

Use explicit storage names: PostgreSQL, MySQL, DynamoDB, Redis, Cassandra, OpenSearch, S3

Avoid generic labels: DB, Storage, Database

Storage labels should indicate purpose (e.g. User PostgreSQL, Order DynamoDB, Search OpenSearch Index, Object Storage S3).

HORIZONTAL SCALING

When scale is mentioned, represent a horizontally scaled tier using ONE node:

[Horizontally Scalable API Services]
[Horizontally Scalable Worker Fleet]
[Horizontally Scalable Executor Workers]

Do not create multiple replica nodes. The rendering system visualizes replicas.

BOTTLENECKS

When scale concerns are discussed, exactly one node may be marked: [Component Name [Bottleneck]]

MICROSERVICE DECOMPOSITION

Each node should have one responsibility: Scheduler, Executor, Metadata Service, Order Service, Notification Service, Search Service

VISUAL PATTERNS

Client → Gateway → Service → Database
Cache above or beside service
Queue below service
Worker below queue
Search Index beside search service
Monitoring above services it observes
Backup below datastore it protects

QUALITY BAR

The diagram should resemble architecture diagrams from AWS Reference Architectures, Uber, Netflix, Stripe, and Tinder Engineering.

Prioritize clarity, visual hierarchy, visual storytelling, logical ownership, and scanability over strict graph-theory optimization.

NODES & SYNTAX

Plain nodes inside layer subgraphs when used.
No nested subgraphs. No classDef.

Short ASCII ids. AWS/cloud services use standard labels.

Generic components:
id[(Label)] = storage
id((Label)) = cache/client/memory
id{Label} = router/filter
id{{Label}} = queue/stream/bus
id[Label] = service/process/API/worker

Only use shapes that match the component purpose.

OUTPUT

First line must be: flowchart LR

Output ONLY Mermaid code. No markdown. No explanations. No prose.

generate.system: Literal HLD architecture diagram generator. Output raw Mermaid flowchart LR only. Visual hierarchy first: layered placement left-to-right (clients → edge → services → storage). Optional top-level layer subgraphs only (Client/Edge/Service/Async/Data) — never nested. Cache beside service, queue/worker below producer. Every arrow labeled. No markdown.
generate.user:
Draw a literal high-level diagram of EXACTLY what the user asked for. Output ONLY valid Mermaid flowchart code.

Do NOT add components or relationships the user did not request. Do NOT complete the design with extras.

The UI renders nodes as icons or labeled boxes. Prioritize visual hierarchy and scanability.

First line MUST be exactly: flowchart LR

{hld_rules}

Example — web client, API gateway, ALB, service with Redis cache, PostgreSQL (layer subgraphs for visual hierarchy):
flowchart LR
    subgraph clients["Client Layer"]
        direction TB
        client((Web Client))
    end
    subgraph edge["Edge Layer"]
        direction TB
        gw[API Gateway]
        alb[Load Balancer]
    end
    subgraph services["Service Layer"]
        direction TB
        svc[Order Service]
        cache((Redis Cache))
    end
    subgraph data["Data Layer"]
        direction TB
        db[(Order PostgreSQL)]
    end
    client -->|HTTPS| gw
    gw --> alb
    alb -->|REST| svc
    svc -->|SQL| db
    svc -->|Redis| cache

Example — job scheduler (async branch from scheduler; worker/queue/Lambda in async layer):
flowchart LR
    subgraph clients["Client Layer"]
        client((Client))
    end
    subgraph edge["Edge Layer"]
        gw[API Gateway]
    end
    subgraph services["Service Layer"]
        scheduler[Scheduler ECS]
    end
    subgraph async["Async Layer"]
        direction TB
        worker[Worker]
        queue{{Job Queue}}
        lambda[AWS Lambda]
    end
    subgraph data["Data Layer"]
        db[(Job DB)]
    end
    client -->|HTTPS| gw
    gw --> scheduler
    scheduler -->|SQL insert| db
    scheduler --> worker
    worker -.->|enqueue| queue
    queue -.->|trigger| lambda
    lambda -->|SQL update| db

USER REQUEST (draw only this — nothing more):
{text_input}

update.system: Literal HLD architecture diagram editor. Update Mermaid flowchart LR. Preserve visual hierarchy: clients left, edge, services, async, storage right. Top-level layer subgraphs allowed; never nested. Cache/queue near owning service. Every arrow labeled. No markdown.
update.user:
Update the architecture diagram per the user's request.

Apply ONLY the changes the user asked for. Do NOT add components or relationships they did not request.
When the user asks to add or remove specific parts, change only those — do not add other "helpful" extras.

Keep flowchart LR. Prioritize visual hierarchy and readability.
Preserve layered layout: Client → Edge → Services → (Async if any) → Data.
Top-level layer subgraphs are allowed for visual boundaries — never nest subgraphs.
Cache beside service; queue/worker below producer service.
Every arrow must stay labeled.

{hld_rules}

CURRENT DIAGRAM:
```
{current_mermaid}
```

USER REQUEST:
{user_prompt}

Output ONLY the complete updated Mermaid flowchart code (flowchart LR).
